title: Information for Debtors
description: Essential information for debtors regarding enforcement proceedings: debtor's obligations, payment methods, enforcement costs, and the bailiff's powers.
content:
1. How to Make Payments to the Bailiff

After enforcement proceedings are initiated, the debtor is obligated to satisfy the debt and pay enforcement costs. All payments to the bailiff must be made:

  • by bank transfer to the bailiff's bank account,
  • in cash on a cash receipt (in person at the office or to the bailiff during field activities).

When making a payment, the case file reference number must be provided.

2. Debtor's Obligations

Every debtor is obliged to inform the bailiff of any change of residence or stay lasting longer than one month, under pain of a fine. If this obligation is neglected, correspondence is left on the case file with the effect of delivery.

3. How to Submit Explanations

The debtor is obliged to submit an explanation regarding their assets.

Pursuant to Art. 801 of the Code of Civil Procedure, if the creditor, or the court ordering enforcement on its own motion, or the authorized authority requesting enforcement does not indicate assets sufficient to satisfy the obligation, the bailiff calls upon the debtor to submit an asset list or other explanations necessary to carry out enforcement.

4. Remember Your Criminal Liability

Criminal liability is incurred pursuant to Art. 300–302 of the Penal Code for:

  • destroying property subject to enforcement,
  • actually or ostensibly encumbering seized or threatened property,
  • removing assets from enforcement proceedings,
  • concealing assets from enforcement,
  • disposing of seized or threatened assets.

5. Can the Bailiff Open Premises and Search Them?

Pursuant to Art. 814 of the Code of Civil Procedure, when required for enforcement purposes, the bailiff may order the opening of the debtor's home and other buildings and premises and search their belongings. If this is insufficient, the bailiff has the right to search clothing worn by the debtor. The bailiff may do so when the debtor attempts to leave or when there is a suspicion that they are trying to conceal items they are carrying.

When carrying out enforcement activities at the debtor's home, if the bailiff sees that the debtor has passed the sought items to a household member or another person for concealment, the bailiff may search that person's clothing and items in their possession.

Searching clothing on the debtor's person and items in their possession outside their home, business, or farm may take place on the basis of written authorisation from the competent President of the District Court. In urgent cases, the search may be carried out without any authorisation; however, the activity must be promptly presented for approval to the President of the District Court. Clothing searches may only be carried out by a person of the same gender as the debtor.

6. Enforcement Costs

Pursuant to Art. 770 of the Act of 17 November 1964 — Code of Civil Procedure, the debtor must reimburse the creditor for costs necessary for the effective conduct of enforcement. Costs are collected together with the enforced claim. Enforcement costs are determined by order of the bailiff.

Pursuant to Art. 7701, a final bailiff's order regarding costs is enforceable upon becoming final without the need for an enforcement clause.

Enforcement costs consist of:

  • enforcement fees,
  • cash expenses.
metaTitle: Information for Debtors | Court Bailiff Harvey Specter
metaDescription: Essential information for debtors: how to make payments, debtor obligations, criminal liability, and the costs of bailiff enforcement.
